Title: A chromosome-scale genome assembly of Artemisia argyi reveals unbiased subgenome evolution and key contributions of gene duplication to volatile terpenoid diversity

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: one of the highly expressed ADS homolog-adjacent genes; analyzed in roots, stems, leaves, and flowers
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: phylogenetic analysis; cloning; heterologous expression in Escherichia coli; catalytic assay; GC-MS
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: does not function as ADS; only catalyzes trace L-elemol production and therefore does not contribute to artemisinin precursor biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: heterologous E. coli system used endogenous FPP
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: not suitable as ADS substitute for artemisinin engineering
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used to test whether A. argyi retained functional ADS homologs; result was negative for artemisinin pathway
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AarTPS76 is an ADS homolog candidate with relatively high expression. 2. Functional testing showed it only generated a trace amount of L-elemol from endogenous FPP in E. coli. 3. AarTPS76 therefore does not support artemisinin precursor formation in A. argyi.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: highly expressed in roots among ADS-homolog-adjacent genes
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: not involved in artemisinin precursor biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: functional divergence away from artemisinin branch
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: not useful as functional ADS replacement
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functionally assayed together with AarTPS76 and AarTPS3 to test ADS-like activity
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AarTPS58 was selected as an ADS-homolog candidate because of sequence similarity and root expression. 2. Functional analyses indicated it was not involved in artemisinin precursor biosynthesis. 3. This supports loss of functional ADS capacity in A. argyi.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: highly expressed in roots among ADS-homolog-adjacent genes; incomplete ORF was also tested
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: did not validate as a functional ADS contributing to artemisinin precursor biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: functional loss/incomplete ORF limits catalytic contribution
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: not useful as artemisinin pathway gene without restoration
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Incomplete open reading frame noted in the article
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AarTPS3 was one of the expressed genes adjacent to ADS homologs. 2. Cloning and catalytic analysis, including the incomplete ORF, did not support ADS function. 3. These data further indicate the absence of functional artemisinin-entry synthase activity in A. argyi.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense and signal transduction for environmental adaptation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: relatively high among GDS homologs; tissue expression profiled in roots, stems, leaves, and flowers
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: germacrene D (major); also δ-elemene, β-ylangene, β-copaene, γ-muurolene, bicyclogermacrene, γ-cadinene/related minor sesquiterpenes, and germacrene D-4-ol as minor products
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes FPP to a broad range of sesquiterpenes with germacrene D as the primary product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key enzyme for volatile terpenoid biosynthesis; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: phylogenetic analysis; expression profiling; recombinant protein enzyme assay; GC-MS product identification; subcellular localization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: promotes production of germacrene D and related sesquiterpenes
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: broad-product enzyme may generate multiple sesquiterpenes rather than a single dedicated product
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: useful for engineering germacrene D-rich volatile profiles in Artemisia or heterologous systems
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Located in a tandem-duplicated GDS gene cluster on chr17; one of six candidate GDS genes in duplicated modules
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AarTPS114 is a highly expressed member of a tandemly duplicated GDS cluster on chr17. 2. Recombinant AarTPS114 converted FPP mainly into germacrene D and several minor sesquiterpenes. 3. AarTPS114 localizes to chloroplasts and is a key contributor to volatile sesquiterpenoid diversity in A. argyi.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: terpene synthase family, likely TPS-a subfamily
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: single-copy GAS retained on chr06; high sequence similarity to GASs from other Asteraceae
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: germacrene A in vivo/in vitro, detected as β-elemene by GC-MS due to thermal Cope rearrangement
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes FPP to germacrene A, which is detected as β-elemene under GC-MS injection conditions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: synteny analysis; recombinant protein enzyme assay with FPP/GPP/GGPP; GC-MS; subcellular localization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: enables specific production of germacrene A/β-elemene branch product
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: single-product specificity may limit volatile diversity relative to promiscuous TPS enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: high GC-MS injection port temperature (250°C) causes germacrene A to rearrange to β-elemene
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: promising single-product enzyme for β-elemene/germacrene A production engineering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Present as a single-copy gene on chr06 and absent from the homologous duplicated segment on chr14
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AarTPS34 is the only retained single-copy GAS in syntenic blocks of A. argyi. 2. It uses FPP, but not GPP or GGPP, to form germacrene A detected as β-elemene by GC-MS. 3. AarTPS34 is chloroplast-localized and represents a specific sesquiterpene synthase branch in A. argyi.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: identified among eight BPPS genes; tissue expression profiled in roots, stems, leaves, and flowers
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: (+)-borneol after dephosphorylation of bornyl diphosphate in assay
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts GPP to bornyl diphosphate, which yields (+)-borneol after dephosphorylation in the enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key enzyme for borneol branch; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: phylogenetic analysis; recombinant enzyme assay; GC-MS product identification
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: produces (+)-borneol as a single product from GPP
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: in vitro assay included alkaline phosphatase dephosphorylation step
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: valuable for metabolic engineering of borneol production
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of eight candidate BPPS genes identified in the genome
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AarTPS89 was identified as a functional BPPS among eight candidate genes. 2. In vitro it produced (+)-borneol as a single product from GPP. 3. AarTPS89 defines the first committed enzymatic step of the borneol/camphor pathway characterized in A. argyi.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: anti-inflammatory, analgesic, antimicrobial, and potential antiviral activities
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: expressed; one of two expressed BDH genes among four key BDH homologs discussed
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes (+)-borneol to (+)-camphor using NAD+ as cofactor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: expression analysis; recombinant enzyme assay; GC-MS product identification; synteny analysis of BDH loci
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: converts borneol to camphor and contributes to camphor biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: useful for engineering or selecting camphor-producing germplasm
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of a tandem-duplicate gene pair and one of the expressed BDHs in A. argyi
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AarBDH4 is an expressed BDH candidate in A. argyi. 2. Functional assays showed that expressed BDHs oxidize (+)-borneol to (+)-camphor using NAD+. 3. AarBDH4 participates in the characterized borneol-to-camphor conversion pathway.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: anti-inflammatory, analgesic, antimicrobial, and potential antiviral activities
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: expressed in A. argyi; functionally characterized
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes (+)-borneol to (+)-camphor using NAD+ as cofactor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: expression analysis; recombinant protein assay; GC-MS product identification; synteny analysis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: candidate enzyme for camphor biosynthesis engineering and quality improvement of medicinal volatiles
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AarBDH5 and AarBDH4 had the same catalytic function; tandem-duplicated genes with different expression patterns were discussed in Discussion
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AarBDH5 is an expressed and functionally validated BDH in A. argyi. 2. It uses (+)-borneol and NAD+ to yield (+)-camphor. 3. Together with AarTPS89, it completes the experimentally characterized borneol/camphor pathway in A. argyi.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: absent; only a partial fragment consisting of one exon and 3' UTR remained on chr05, none on chr13
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: present as tandemly duplicated gene cluster with six or four copies in two A. annua strains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes FPP to amorpha-4,11-diene, the precursor of artemisinin
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key and rate-limiting enzyme for artemisinin biosynthesis as stated in Discussion
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: comparative synteny analysis; gene structure comparison; absence/presence analysis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: absence in A. argyi explains lack of artemisinin production
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: loss of artemisinin branch in A. argyi
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: restoration or introduction of ADS would be essential for engineering artemisinin production in A. argyi
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a cross-species core pathway reference because absence in A. argyi was experimentally analyzed in Results
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The A. annua ADS cluster is syntenically missing from A. argyi, where only a partial ADS fragment remains. 2. ADS is the committed, rate-limiting enzyme for artemisinin biosynthesis in A. annua. 3. Its absence in A. argyi likely underlies the lack of artemisinin production.
